content.label: Essential Question
content.question:
2008 Financial Crisis: When the Economy Nearly Collapsed

What Happened: Bear Stearns collapses (March 2008), Lehman Brothers fails (Sept 2008), Stock market crashes 40%, Unemployment: 5% → 10%, Home values drop 30%

What Government Did: Federal Reserve cut rates to 0% and bought $4 trillion in bonds. Congress passed $700B bank bailout + $800B stimulus.

Today's goal: Understand HOW government tries to stabilize the economy and WHETHER it works!

objectives.description: fiscal policy tools including government spending, taxation, and budget deficits
monetary policy tools including the Federal Reserve, interest rates, and money supply management
how fiscal and monetary policies affect employment, inflation, and economic growth
appropriate policy responses to recessions and inflation

terms.definition: Government's use of spending and taxation to influence the economy
terms.example: COVID stimulus checks, infrastructure spending bills

terms.definition: Federal Reserve managing interest rates and money supply to stabilize the economy
terms.example: Fed raising rates to fight inflation in 2022-2024

terms.definition: Interest rate banks charge each other for overnight loans—influences ALL other interest rates
terms.example: Currently 5.25-5.50%, affects mortgage and credit card rates

terms.definition: When government spends more than it collects in taxes in a given year
terms.example: Current federal deficit: ~$2 trillion per year

terms.definition: Policies that stimulate economic growth: more spending, lower taxes, lower interest rates
terms.example: Used during recessions to create jobs and boost GDP

terms.definition: Policies that cool down the economy: less spending, higher taxes, higher interest rates
terms.example: Used to fight inflation—Fed raising rates in 2022

layoutData.title: Economies naturally go through cycles of expansion and contraction
paragraphs: 1. Expansion: GDP growing, unemployment falling, consumer confidence high
2. Peak: Maximum output, inflation may start rising
3. Recession: GDP shrinking for 2+ quarters, unemployment rising
4. Trough: Lowest point with highest unemployment before recovery begins
bulletPoints: Cycles typically last 5-10 years from peak to peak
Government tries to smooth cycles—stimulate during downturns, cool during booms
No two cycles are identical in length or severity
layoutData.keyPoint: Countercyclical Policy: Act OPPOSITE to the cycle—stimulate during recession, cool during boom to reduce volatility

leftPanel.title: Government Spending (~$6 trillion/year)
paragraphs: Effect: ↑ Spending → More jobs created → GDP increases

highlightBox.text: Government is the single largest spender in the economy

infoCard.title: Taxation Breakdown
items: Effect: ↓ Taxes → More disposable income → GDP increases

layoutData.title: Each $1 of government spending generates $1.50-2.00 of total economic activity
paragraphs: Step 1: Construction workers earn income from road projects
Step 2: Workers spend paychecks at stores and restaurants
Step 3: Those businesses hire more workers with increased revenue
Step 4: New workers spend their income, continuing the cycle
bulletPoints: Multiplier works in reverse too—spending cuts reduce GDP by more than the cut
Size of multiplier depends on how much people save vs. spend
Direct spending (roads, schools) has larger multiplier than tax cuts
layoutData.keyPoint: Key Question: Why not unlimited spending? Answer: Inflation, growing deficits, and unsustainable debt burden!

highlightBox.text: Deficit = annual shortfall; Debt = total accumulated deficits over time

infoCard.title: The Debate
items: • Can pay back during boom times

leftColumn.title: Expansionary (Used During Recession)
items: Example: 2020 COVID response—$5 trillion in stimulus spending and tax cuts

rightColumn.title: Contractionary (Used During Inflation)
items: Reality: Rarely used—politicians don't like cutting spending or raising taxes!

leftPanel.title: What Is the Fed?
paragraphs: • Independent central bank (not part of government)
• Not controlled by Congress or President
• Can make unpopular decisions (like raising rates)
• Protects from political pressure for short-term gains

highlightBox.text: Fed independence is crucial for credible monetary policy

infoCard.title: Dual Mandate
items: Keep unemployment as low as possible
The Challenge: These goals sometimes conflict! Lowering unemployment can increase inflation, and vice versa.

content.question:
Should the Federal Reserve be more accountable to elected officials?

Consider: The Fed makes decisions affecting millions of jobs and everyone's purchasing power, yet is not controlled by Congress or the President. Is this independence essential for good policy, or does it give too much power to unelected officials?

content.headerTitle: The Federal Funds Rate: Most Important Tool

layoutData.title: Federal Funds Rate = Interest rate banks charge each other for overnight loans
paragraphs: This influences ALL other interest rates in the economy
🏠 Mortgages | 🚗 Auto Loans | 💳 Credit Cards | 🎓 Student Loans | 🏦 Savings Rates
Affects everyone's borrowing, saving, and spending decisions
Ultimately affects GDP, employment, and inflation
bulletPoints: When Fed raises rates, borrowing becomes more expensive throughout economy
When Fed lowers rates, borrowing becomes cheaper to stimulate activity
Changes take 6-18 months to have full effect on economy
layoutData.keyPoint: The federal funds rate is the most powerful economic policy tool in existence

content.headerTitle: How Rate Changes Affect the Economy

content.question:
2008 Crisis: Did government do too much, too little, or just right?

Think about: Were bank bailouts fair to taxpayers? Did massive spending and rate cuts prevent a depression? Did we create 'moral hazard' by bailing out bad actors? What should government have done differently?

leftColumn.title: Response to Recession
items: • Quantitative easing if rates already at 0%
Example: 2020 COVID—$5T fiscal stimulus + Fed cut rates to 0%

rightColumn.title: Response to High Inflation
items: • Accept slower growth and higher unemployment
Example: 2022-2024—Fed raised rates from 0% to 5.5% to fight inflation

questions.question: What is the difference between fiscal policy and monetary policy? Who controls each?
Explain how the federal funds rate affects the entire economy. Give three specific examples of rates it influences.
If the economy enters a recession, what specific actions should the Federal Reserve and Congress take? Why?
Why is contractionary fiscal policy (cutting spending, raising taxes) rarely used even during high inflation?

takeaways.title: Business cycles are natural
takeaways.description: Economies go through expansion, peak, recession, and trough—government tries to smooth these cycles

takeaways.title: Fiscal policy uses spending and taxes
takeaways.description: Controlled by Congress and President—powerful but slow and politically difficult

takeaways.title: Monetary policy uses interest rates
takeaways.description: Controlled by independent Federal Reserve—faster and less political than fiscal policy

takeaways.title: Federal funds rate is the key tool
takeaways.description: Influences all other interest rates—affects borrowing, saving, spending, and investment decisions

takeaways.title: Expansionary policies stimulate growth
takeaways.description: Used during recessions: increase spending, cut taxes, lower interest rates

takeaways.title: Contractionary policies fight inflation
takeaways.description: Cool economy: decrease spending, raise taxes, raise interest rates—politically unpopular

main.title: Tomorrow's Learning Lab
main.description: Apply your knowledge by simulating Fed Chair decisions and analyzing real policy impacts

steps: Analyze Federal Reserve meeting minutes and rate decisions
Evaluate government budget priorities and trade-offs
Simulate policy responses to different economic scenarios
Assess how current fiscal and monetary policy affects YOUR personal finances
Play 'Fed Chair for a Day' and defend your policy decisions

content.tagline: "Understanding the tools government uses to manage the economy"
